channel.title: Boston Women in Bioinformatics's Events
channel.description: Stay updated with our upcoming events and meetups

item.title: Nextflow Core Hackathon
item.content:encoded:
When: Wednesday, March 11, 2026 at 2:00 PM

Where: CambridgeSide Mall, 100 Cambridgeside Pl, Cambridge, Massachusetts

Details

Join nextflow enthusiasts in person or online March 11-13, 2026, for the nf-core hackathon! See updates to the Boston site here.

Registration

Please register in both places:

  1. Register for the hackathon on the official nf-core site to join the event and get access to project channels.
  2. Register for the Boston in-person site on Luma to reserve your spot at the physical location.

What is the NF-Core Hackathon?

nf-core is a community effort to collect a curated set of tools, modules, and pipelines built using Nextflow. Whether you're already involved in nf-core or not, this hackathon is the perfect place to get to know other members of the community in person. You'll collaborate on projects, discuss code, and learn from each other.

To get a feel for how the event works, check out the similar-style hackathons held in 2024 and 2025.

How It Works

The nf-core hackathons are not competitive — they're collaborative, community-driven events where participants work together on projects. Projects can be anything from adding new features to existing pipelines to creating entirely new ones. You can bring your own topic or choose from a list of open issues in the community. We group people based on the types of tasks being tackled, so you can work with others who have similar interests.

Who Should Attend?

All are welcome — no prior nf-core contributions needed. Newcomers to nf-core are encouraged to join, but some experience writing Nextflow code is expected. This is a hackathon, not a training workshop.

Key dates

  • ​Registration: Open now
  • ​Hackathon: March 11–13, 2026

How to find us

Address: CambridgeSide Mall, 100 CambridgeSide Pl, Cambridge, MA 02141 — Second floor conference room

Getting there:

  • By T: Take the MBTA Green Line to Lechmere Station, then walk ~5 minutes to the mall entrance.
  • By car: The mall has a parking garage with hourly rates. Enter from First Street or Land Boulevard.

Once inside:

  1. Head up to the second floor
  2. Find TJ Maxx as your landmark
  3. From TJ Maxx walk toward the Craft Loft
  4. The conference room will be on left of Craft Loft — look for signs

item.title: Byte and Bite: Bioinformatics Lunch Meetup
item.content:encoded:
When: Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6:00 PM

Where: Shy Bird - Kendall Square, 390 Third St, Cambridge, Massachusetts

Details

​Celebrate the arrival of spring with your fellow bioinformatics besties! It’s been six weeks since Groundhog Day, which means winter is finally behind us. What better excuse to grab lunch together?!

​Whether you're already part of our community or hoping to make new connections, join us for a relaxed lunch where women and all gender minorities can meet, mingle, and share ideas over good food and great conversation. Let’s shake off the winter chill and step into the new season with inspiring discussions, meaningful networking, and a little springtime spirit.

What to Know Before You Register

  • This gathering is intended for individuals who identify as women. 👩‍🔬
  • Each guest will be responsible for purchasing their own meal and/or beverages. 🍽️

How to find us

For those driving, parking is available at the 350 Kendall Street Garage (Pilgrim Parking). If you’re taking the T, Shy Bird is just a short walk from the Kendall/MIT station.

item.title: From Counts to Cell Type — A 2-Day Hands-On scRNA-seq Workshop
item.content:encoded:
When: Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 5:00 PM

Where: Zoom

Have you read single-cell RNA-seq papers but never run the analysis yourself? This beginner-friendly workshop will take you from raw 10x Genomics data to identifying real biological cell types.

Hosted by Boston Women in Bioinformatics (BWiB) and sponsored by Sprout Informatics, this 2-day live Zoom series is designed for students and early-stage researchers ready to get hands-on with single-cell analysis.

What you’ll do:

  • Understand how single-cell RNA-seq experiments work
  • Load a real PBMC dataset from 10x Genomics
  • Perform quality control (UMIs, gene counts, mitochondrial %)
  • Normalize and cluster cells
  • Identify marker genes
  • Determine the most abundant cell type — and explain why

Day 1 — April 2

Understanding the experiment, connecting it to the gene-by-cell count matrix, setting up the analysis environment, and performing QC and filtering.

Day 2 — April 3

From clean data to biological insight: normalization, dimensionality reduction (PCA/UMAP), clustering, marker gene analysis, and assigning cell identities.

By the end of the workshop, you will confidently interpret a UMAP and defend your biological conclusion.

Who is this for?

  • Beginners to scRNA-seq
  • Students looking for structured, hands-on training
  • Researchers who want to move from theory to practice

Summary

  • Format: Live on Zoom
  • Registration: $10 (covers compute infrastructure)
    • Fee waived upon assignment submission
  • Spots may be limited due to compute capacity.

Join us as we bridge experiment to analysis — and turn counts into cell types.
